###### [Identity](/us/basics/identity)

# Normalize Identity Expression with the Neopronoun Revolution

## Embracing neopronouns might personalize and revolutionize identity expression.

Posted May 28, 2025

|
[Reviewed by Lybi Ma](/us/docs/editorial-process)

![Share on Facebook](https://cdn2.psychologytoday.com/theme-assets/icons/icon-share-round-facebook-md.svg)
![Share on X](https://cdn2.psychologytoday.com/theme-assets/icons/icon-share-round-twitter-md.svg)
![Share on Bluesky](https://cdn2.psychologytoday.com/theme-assets/icons/icon-share-round-bluesky-blue.svg)
![Share on LinkedIn](https://cdn2.psychologytoday.com/theme-assets/icons/icon-share-round-linkedin-md.svg)
![Share via Email](https://cdn2.psychologytoday.com/theme-assets/icons/icon-share-round-email-md.svg)

### Key points

In recent years, the conversation around personal pronouns has gained unprecedented traction, reshaping how we navigate [identity](https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/basics/identity "Psychology Today looks at identity") and communication. Pronouns—those small, seemingly innocuous words like *he, she,* or *they*—have become a cultural flashpoint, signaling deeper shifts in our understanding of [gender](https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/basics/gender "Psychology Today looks at gender") and individuality. While traditional pronouns have long served as linguistic shorthand, a new wave of [innovation](https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/basics/creativity "Psychology Today looks at innovation") has emerged: neopronouns. These novel pronouns, such as *xe-xem, zir-zirs*, or even *ey-em,* offer a radical opportunity to reimagine how we address one another. By embracing neopronouns en masse, society can normalize pronoun use, fostering a world where identity is fluid, inclusive, and endlessly customizable. But what does this mean for our collective psyche, and why should we champion this linguistic evolution?

**The Psychological Case for Neopronouns**

At its core, language shapes our reality. Psychological research, including studies from the field of sociolinguistics, demonstrates that the words we use influence how we perceive ourselves and others. When we adopt pronouns that align with an individual’s identity, we validate their sense of self, reducing [cognitive dissonance](https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/basics/cognitive-dissonance "Psychology Today looks at cognitive dissonance") and fostering psychological well-being. Neopronouns take this a step further by breaking free from the binary constraints of *he* and *she*, offering a linguistic palette as diverse as human experience itself. For example, someone identifying as [nonbinary](https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/basics/transgender "Psychology Today looks at nonbinary") might find *they-them* insufficiently specific, whereas *ze-zir* feels uniquely affirming. By normalizing neopronouns, we create a culture where everyone’s identity is seen and respected—a powerful antidote to the alienation many feel in a rigid, binary world.

Moreover, neopronouns encourage cognitive flexibility. Learning new pronouns requires mental agility, forcing us to rethink assumptions about gender and identity. This aligns with research on neuroplasticity, which suggests that challenging our linguistic habits can enhance cognitive adaptability. Imagine a workplace where colleagues introduce themselves with pronouns like *fae-faer* or *per-pers*. The initial discomfort of learning these terms could spark a broader [openness](https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/basics/openness "Psychology Today looks at openness") to diverse perspectives, fostering empathy and [collaboration](https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/basics/teamwork "Psychology Today looks at collaboration"). Over time, this practice could rewire our social interactions, making inclusivity second nature.

**Why Mass [Adoption](https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/basics/adoption "Psychology Today looks at Adoption") Matters**

The push for neopronouns isn’t just about individual validation—it’s about transforming societal norms. If everyone adopted neopronouns, pronoun use would cease to be a niche concern for marginalized groups and become a universal practice. Picture a world where every email signature includes a unique pronoun set, from *xe-xem* to *noun-nouns* or even *star-stars*. This universal adoption would erase the [stigma](https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/basics/mental-health-stigma "Psychology Today looks at stigma") associated with pronoun declarations, making them as routine as stating one’s name. By democratizing pronoun use, we dismantle the hierarchy that privileges traditional pronouns and marginalizes those who deviate from the norm.

Mass adoption also has practical benefits. In a globalized society, where interactions span cultures and languages, neopronouns offer a standardized yet customizable framework. Unlike traditional pronouns, which vary widely across languages (gendered pronouns in Spanish or French), neopronouns are often designed to be gender-neutral and adaptable. If we all embraced pronouns like *ey-em* or invented our own—say, *bloom-blooms* for someone who identifies with growth and renewal—we could create a universal pronoun protocol, streamlining communication in an increasingly interconnected world.

**Addressing the Skeptics**

Of course, neopronouns face resistance. Critics argue that they’re confusing, impractical, or even unnecessary. “Why complicate language?” they ask. “Isn’t *they-them* enough?” These objections, while understandable, miss the point. Neopronouns aren’t about complexity for complexity’s sake; they’re about precision. Just as we’ve expanded our vocabulary to describe emotions (from “sad” to “melancholy” or “ennui”), neopronouns allow us to articulate identity with nuance. If someone identifies as *void-voids* to reflect a sense of cosmic detachment, who are we to deny them that specificity?

Skeptics also worry about the cognitive load of memorizing countless pronoun sets. But this concern overlooks our remarkable capacity for adaptation. We already navigate complex social cues—think of the mental gymnastics required to remember names, titles, or dietary preferences at a dinner party. Adding pronouns like *kit-kits* or *wave-waves* to the mix is a small price to pay for a more inclusive society. If we can memorize wi-fi passwords, we can certainly handle a few extra syllables.

**The Future of Pronouns: A Linguistic Free-for-All**

To truly normalize pronoun use, we can go beyond accommodation and embrace innovation. Why stop at a handful of neopronouns? Let’s encourage everyone to craft their own, reflecting their unique essence. Imagine a world where pronouns are as personal as fingerprints: *cloud-clouds* for the dreamer, *spark-sparks* for the innovator, or even *byte-bytes* for the tech enthusiast. [Social media](https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/basics/social-media "Psychology Today looks at Social media") platforms could integrate pronoun generators, offering algorithms to suggest pronouns based on [personality](https://www.psychologytoday.com/us/basics/personality "Psychology Today looks at personality") quizzes or aesthetic preferences. The result: A linguistic landscape where every interaction is a celebration of individuality.

This vision, of course, requires commitment. Schools could teach neopronoun conjugation alongside traditional grammar, ensuring fluency from a young age. Businesses could mandate pronoun workshops, where employees brainstorm their own pronoun sets in team-building exercises. Governments could issue pronoun passports, allowing citizens to update their pronouns as often as their moods dictate. The administrative burden would be significant, but the payoff—a society where no one feels unseen—would be worth it.

![](https://cdn2.psychologytoday.com/assets/styles/manual_crop_1_1_75x75/public/teaser_image/blog_entry/2025-08/IMG_6575.JPG.jpg?itok=LMTOGYWC "Sophia Dembling")
![](https://cdn2.psychologytoday.com/assets/styles/manual_crop_1_1_75x75/public/teaser_image/blog_entry/2025-08/library-7408106_1280.jpg?itok=EW3HMUYq "wal_172619 / Pixabay")

**A Call to Action**

The case for neopronouns is clear: they promote psychological well-being, enhance cognitive flexibility, and pave the way for a more inclusive world. By adopting neopronouns en masse, we can normalize pronoun use, stripping away the controversy and making identity expression a universal practice. Let’s lean into this linguistic revolution. Introduce yourself with *xe-xem* at your next meeting. Experiment with *glint-glints* on your social media bio. Encourage your friends to invent pronouns like *mirth-mirths* or *whisper-whispers*. Together, we can build a world where pronouns are as boundless as our imaginations—and where no one is ever misnamed.

References

Barnes, M. M. (2023). *The politics of neopronouns: a comparative study of pronoun use amongst nonbinary language users in peninsular Spanish and British English* (Doctoral dissertation, University of Birmingham).

Berger, J., Cohen, B. P., & Zelditch Jr, M. (1972). Status characteristics and social interaction. *American sociological review*, 241-255.

Bertulfo, C. (2018). Beyond He and She: A Study on the Non-Binary and Gender Neutral English Neopronouns. *Available at SSRN 4783597*.

Boroditsky, L. (2011). How language shapes thought. *Scientific American*, *304*(2), 62-65.

Gustafsson Sendén, M., Bäck, E. A., & Lindqvist, A. (2015). Introducing a gender-neutral pronoun in a natural gender language: the influence of time on attitudes and behavior. *Frontiers in psychology*, *6*, 893.

Matsuno, E., & Budge, S. L. (2017). Non-binary/genderqueer identities: A critical review of the literature. *Current Sexual Health Reports*, *9*, 116-120.

Renström, E. A. (2025). The implementation of neo-and nonbinary pronouns: a review of current research and future challenges. *Frontiers in Psychology*, *15*, 1507858.

St Amant, M., Cai, J., Rider, G. N., & Lee, R. (2025). Nonbinary identity and pronoun use: A qualitative analysis. *International Journal of Transgender Health*, *26*(2), 413-427.

Zimman, L. (2017). Transgender language reform. *Journal of Language and Discrimination*, *1*(1), 84-105.

FULL CONTENT:
### CNN values your feedback

# A guide to neopronouns, from ae to ze

![Scottie Andrew](https://media.cnn.com/api/v1/images/stellar/prod/1476650-4926-r-v1-ps2560x1440.jpg?c=16x9&q=h_270,w_480,c_fill/c_thumb,g_face,w_100,h_100)
![Neopronouns like xe, ze or em are nonbinary descriptors.](https://media.cnn.com/api/v1/images/stellar/prod/230808104424-neopronouns-stock.jpg?c=16x9&q=h_833,w_1480,c_fill)

*He’s* my boss; *her* dog is cute; *they*have an exam today — pronouns are a part of speech we use to refer to ourselves and others. They’re an essential component of language — and, as of the last few years, among its most [hotly contested,](https://www.cnn.com/2021/03/29/us/ohio-professor-transgender-appeals-trnd/index.html) too.

Some of the most common words in the English language have gender markers, including pronouns. But not all of them are binary. Consider the [singular “they,”](https://www.cnn.com/2021/05/19/us/gender-pronouns-wellness-explainer-trnd/index.html) preferred by some nonbinary and trans people for whom gendered pronouns do not fit.

And then there are neopronouns (“new” pronouns), gender-neutral or nonbinary pronouns that are distinctfrom the common she, he and they. Neopronouns include terms like “xe” and “em,” and some of them even date back several centuries, when they were introduced by writers as a solution for referring to subjects without assuming gender. Now, they’re alsocommonly used by nonbinary and trans people.

All pronouns indicate identity and can be used to include or exclude people they describe — neopronouns included, said Dennis Baron, one of the foremost experts on neopronouns and their histories and an emeritus professor of English and Linguisticsat the University of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign. Neopronouns should be used and respected like any other pronoun, he told CNN.

“People like to have a say in how they’re identified,” Baron said. “Refusing to let people self-identify is a way of excluding them.”

## Neopronouns, explained

The most common third-person pronouns include “she,” “he” and “they.” While “she” and “he” are typically used as gendered pronouns to refer to a woman and a man respectively, “they” can be used as a gender-neutral descriptor for an individual person or a group of people. Celebrities like [Janelle Monáe](https://www.cnn.com/2022/04/22/entertainment/janelle-monae-nonbinary-cec/index.html), [Emma Corrin](https://www.cnn.com/2022/11/27/entertainment/emma-corrin-gender-neutral-awards-categories/index.html) and [Jonathan Van Ness](https://www.nbcnews.com/nbc-out/out-pop-culture/jonathan-van-ness-wants-stay-curious-new-netflix-series-rcna13958) have each said “they” is a pronoun that works for them.

Neopronouns, meanwhile, are less commonly used than those three familiar pronouns. They’re often used by nonbinary, transgender and gender nonconforming people because they offer more freedom of identity. In his book “What’s Your Pronoun?” Baron wrote that neopronouns “expand the ways that people are able to indicate their gender identity to encompass anyone who is trans or nonbinary, as well as those who choose an altogether different term to characterize their gender.”

Per the LGBTQ advocacy group the Human Rights Campaign, neopronouns are a “step towards a society where people can more fully express all parts of themselves.”

Some neopronouns were created by writers as far back as the 18th century, many of whom did not publicly identify as nonbinary, because they wanted a genderless word to describe a person or group of people —only recently have pronouns been used as a political toolfor the way they’re used by nonbinary and trans people, Baron wrote. Other neopronouns are completely original to their user – some may choose to select a noun to describe themselves, like “star” or “starself” in place of binarypronouns like “she” or “herself.” These are called nounself neopronouns, but more on those later.

## How to use neopronouns

Here are a few relatively common neopronouns, and how to use them, from the [Human Rights Campaign](https://www.hrc.org/resources/understanding-neopronouns). Note: Some of these pronouns may be pronounced differently based on their user.

**xe/xyr** *(commonly [pronounced](https://www.mykidisgay.com/blog/defining-neopronouns) **zee**/**zeer**)*

I asked **xyr** to come to the movies. **Xe** said yes!

**ze/zir** or**ze/hir**(*commonly [pronounced](https://pronouns.org/ze-hir) **zee/zeer**or **zee/heer***)

The teacher graded **zir**paper today, and **ze**got an A!

**Ze** said **hirself** that I’m **hir** favorite neighbor.

**fae/faer** *(commonly pronounced****fay/fair****)*

**Fae** told me that **faer** best friend is in town this week.

**ey/em/eir** *(*[*commonly*](https://www.lgbtqandall.com/how-to-practice-using-neopronouns/)[*pronounced*](https://www.american.edu/student-affairs/cdi/upload/pronouns-explained.pdf)***aye/em/air****)*

I’m taking **em** to the park today. **Ey** wants to bring **eir** camera to capture the garden for **emself**!

**ae/aer** *(commonly [pronounced](https://www.tiktok.com/@lesbiansnowwhite/video/7169427838343335211?lang=en) aye/air)*

**Ae** is my best friend — most of **aer’s** weekday evenings are spent at my house.

## Why people use neopronouns

People may use neopronouns for the same reason someone else uses “she” and/or “they” — neopronouns may better align with one’s identity. Some people may use a common pronoun, like “she” or “they,” in addition to a neopronoun.

Neopronouns are ultimately a “reflection of (someone’s) personal identity,” [according](https://www.hrc.org/resources/understanding-neopronouns) to the Human Rights Campaign, and thus the “number and types of neopronouns a person may use (are) limitless.”

Dua Saleh, a musician and actor who’s appeared on the Netflix hit “Sex Education,” uses the pronouns they and xe. Saleh [told](https://twitter.com/doitlikedua/status/1315366612598480897?lang=en) their social media followers in 2020, after xestarted to use xe pronouns, that it’s “really affirming to find the pronouns that are right for you.”

“I just like the neopronouns,” Saleh [told](https://www.complex.com/music/a/loudelaney/dua-saleh) Complex in 2022. “I feel like they fit me better, not all the time, but they’re just fitting. There’s an element where I’m just like, ‘Oh, this sounds really nice.’ Or it sounds nice coming out of my mouth or hearing other people say it.”

Trans and nonbinary people may use neopronouns because binary pronouns like “he” and “she” don’t align with their identities, but neopronouns aren’t exclusive to LGBTQ and nonbinary people. Gender identity and pronouns can be [personal](https://pronouns.org/neopronouns), and asking someone what their pronouns are and how they identify may be considered intrusive in some contexts, like if a person is not out, or does not have an established trust with the person asking. But if someone shares that they use neopronouns, it’s important to use those pronouns when you refer to them. In many circles, it is considered polite to offer one’s own pronouns before asking someone else for theirs.

“People who aren’t used to thinking about what pronouns others use to refer to themselves might make assumptions about someone else’s pronouns based on their name or appearance,” CNN’s Harmeet Kaur [wrote](https://www.cnn.com/2021/05/19/us/gender-pronouns-wellness-explainer-trnd/index.html) in 2021. “But those assumptions can be incorrect and similarly hurtful because they imply a person has to look a certain way to demonstrate their gender identity.”

“It’s polite to honor a person’s identity when speaking to them or about them,” Baron told CNN. “Using inclusive language, not just inclusive pronouns but other self-identifiers as well, can facilitate discourse by showing respect.” Examples of those self-identifiers can include terms like “parent” instead of “mother” or “father,” or “sibling” instead of “sister” or “brother.”

## Neopronouns have a long history

Baron said that he’s found over 200 neopronoun “coinages,” many of which were created before the 20th century, more than 120 years before the “current pronoun debates.”

Previous pronoun debates revolved around the non-inclusivity of using “he” as a generic pronoun (as in the Bible: “He that is without sin among you, let him be the first to cast a stone”), because “he” often only referred to men, Baron told CNN. The issue became more pressing when the women’s rights movement gained traction in the 19th century.

And so, linguists and other intellectuals started proposing new “generic” pronouns that could be used to describe people without specifying their genders. Some of them were created from existing English words, like “ou,” repurposed from a term in some dialects in England, or from other languages, like “le,” which in French means “the.”

And then there were words that Baron said filled a “semantic black hole,” words that didn’t exist until they were invented, likely, just for this purpose. For example, “heer,” “hiser” and “himer” were [proposed](https://blogs.illinois.edu/view/25/612401423#:~:text=Ella%20Young%20Invents%20Pronoun%20.%20.%20.,The%20story%20went%20viral.) by Ella Young, the first woman superintendent of Chicago public schools, in 1912, he said. “The English language is in need of a personal pronoun of the third person, singular number, that will indicate both sexes,” Young told Chicago Tribunereporters at the time.

Referring to some words, such as “hir” and “ze,” as “neopronouns” may not even be technically correct, Baron wrote in “What’s Your Pronoun?” Both of those examples are over 100 years old: “Hir” was used in the Sacramento Bee in 1920, and “ze” was used by a writer known only by the initials JWL in 1864. (JWL proposed that the gender-neutral “ze” find a place in Noah Webster’s early dictionaries; Webster died before the piece on “ze” could reach him, Baron [wrote](https://blogs.illinois.edu/view/25/705317) in 2018.)

But the neopronoun “thon”*did* [make it into the Merriam-Webster dictionary](https://www.merriam-webster.com/wordplay/third-person-gender-neutral-pronoun-thon) in 1934, defined as “a proposed genderless pronoun of the third person.” Baron wrote that the composer and lawyer Charles Crozat Converse introduced the pronoun “thon,” a contraction of “that one,” in the mid-to-late 19th century but didn’t start promoting its use until 1884, when critics and editorial writers debated whether it would resonate with readers. It was dropped from Webster’s dictionary in 1961, Baron [wrote](https://blogs.illinois.edu/view/25/597154), after failing to widely catch on.

Baron and other language historians credit some internet communities with proliferating neopronouns in the late 20th and early 21st centuries. The mathematician Michael Spivak used a variety of preexisting gender-neutral neopronouns in his 1990 software guide “The Joy of TeX,” which were also used by many members of LambdaMOO, an early online program where users could choose pronouns, including the “Spivak pronouns” e, em, eir and emself, to match their avatars.

Even though there were dozens of neopronouns that made it into print in the 19th and 20th centuries, most of them didn’t make a huge impact upon their introduction or were lost to time, Baron said. During that time, the public was resistant to language change, and men in positions of power often didn’t take issue with a lack of gender-neutral pronouns, Baron wrote.

Today, people’s resistance to neopronouns likely stems from their use by nonbinary and trans people, Baron said. The rights of trans people, particularly youth, have come under attack in state legislatures in [more than half of US states](https://www.cnn.com/2023/03/13/politics/joe-biden-daily-show-trans-rights/index.html) in the last year, attempting to restrict them from access to gender-affirming health care, playing school sports or their [ability to change their gender markers](https://www.cnn.com/2023/07/11/us/kansas-transgender-drivers-license-law/index.html) on legal documents. Understanding and using someone’s pronouns is one way to show solidarity and respect toward trans people.

## An intro to nounself neopronouns

*Leaf, sun, star* — [nounself pronouns](https://chss.wwu.edu/linguistics/aspen-clark-nounself-pronouns-101) are neopronouns that use nature and other inspirations as nonbinary or genderless descriptors. Linguist Jason D’Angelo [told](https://www.nytimes.com/2021/04/08/style/neopronouns-nonbinary-explainer.html) The New York Times that nounself pronouns were popularized on the social platform Tumblr around 2012 and 2013 and remain in use among members of fandoms who may take their nounself pronouns from the properties they enjoy.

For someone who uses the nounself pronoun “leaf,” that may look like: “I hope **leaf**knows how proud we are that **leaf**is getting to know **leafself**better!” or “**Leaf**arrived at the coffee shop before me; I was mortified to have been late to meet **leaf**.”

In a 2016 [paper](https://tidsskrift.dk/lwo/article/view/23431/20535) on the emerging pronouns, Danish linguist Ehm Hjorth Miltersen wrote that nounself pronouns offer a way for people to establish identity beyond just gender. By finding one’s desired nounself pronouns, one can “can construct new ways to identify and be perceived by others that are more coordinate with complex and diverse identities.” Miltersen wrote that one nounself pronoun user who responded to their questionnaire wrote that they sometimes use “pup/pupself” pronouns to “express a level of fun, happiness and excitement … in me.”

Some critics of nounself pronouns feel that the words sound “silly” or “make it harder for transgender and nonbinary people to be taken seriously” since the terms are often much newer and less commonly used than other pronouns, Miltersen said.

Baron noted, though, that all words, in any language, were “invented” at one point.

“Like it or not, lots of new words pop up every day,” Baron said.

--- SOURCE 5: Neopronouns - Are You Up On This New Evolution of English ... ---
URL: https://www.leewind.org/neopronouns-are-you-up-on-this-new-evolution-of-english-language/

SUMMARY:
Noun-self pronouns. Words created to be pronouns that are not gendered. As reported by Ezra Marcus in the New York Times, “A Guide to Neopronouns“.

FULL CONTENT:
![Lee Wind](https://www.leewind.org/wp-content/uploads/2021/03/le-wind-web-header@2x.png)

# Neopronouns – Are You Up On This New Evolution of English-Language Terms of Identity?

![A screen shot of the New York Times Article "A Guide to Neopronouns" by Ezra Marcus showing an illustration self-portrait by @katetorias with the words Pri/Prin/Prins/Princeself next to Princeself. Pri is shown with tan skin, green hair, white glasses, a gold crown against a pink background.](https://www.leewind.org/wp-content/uploads/2021/04/NYTimes-NeoPronouns.png "NYTimes-NeoPronouns")

Noun-self pronouns. Words created to be pronouns that are not gendered. As reported by Ezra Marcus in the *New York Times*, “[A Guide to Neopronouns](https://www.nytimes.com/2021/04/08/style/neopronouns-nonbinary-explainer.html)“

![](https://www.leewind.org/wp-content/uploads/2021/04/NYTimes-NeoPronouns-783x1024.png)

I learned so much reading it, including the fact that in a [survey by the Trevor Project](https://www.thetrevorproject.org/2020/07/29/research-brief-pronouns-usage-among-lgbtq-youth/) of 40,000 Queer Youth on pronoun use,

found that one-quarter of them used nonbinary pronouns. (Participants were recruited from late 2019 through early 2020 by ads on social media.) Most said they used common pronouns like “he,” “she” and “they.”

Just 4 percent said they used neopronouns, including “ze/zir,” and “fae/faer,” often in combination with other pronouns.

[The article is well-worth reading!](https://www.nytimes.com/2021/04/08/style/neopronouns-nonbinary-explainer.html) And it’s a good reminder that language exists to serve us, so we can express ourselves. As such, it evolves. And that’s a good thing.

![](https://www.leewind.org/wp-content/uploads/2021/04/Another-NeoPronoun-SelfPortrait-by-Anna-Stevens-Hill-1024x779.png)

*The light in me recognizes and acknowledges the light in you, no matter your pronoun, or neopronoun, or if you choose to not use any pronoun at all.*  
Lee

enriched_points.expanded_content: Neopronouns, such as 'xe' and 'ze', represent a linguistic shift allowing individuals to express gender identities beyond traditional binaries, promoting inclusivity in language.
The emergence of neopronouns illustrates language's adaptability, reflecting evolving societal views on gender, identity, and the importance of personalized self-expression.
Neopronouns are commonly used in online spaces, fostering safe environments for individuals to explore and affirm their identities, enhancing community support and understanding.
In digital communities, neopronouns facilitate acceptance and validation, serving as tools for individuals to communicate their identities, fostering connection and inclusivity.
The usage of neopronouns contributes significantly to discussions surrounding gender identity, challenging traditional norms and encouraging broader recognition of diverse gender expressions.
